title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and daily plastic waste generation for countries in 2010, plotting GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis against waste in kilograms per person per day on the vertical axis. The data points, color-coded by continent and sized by population, show no single linear progression; instead, high waste generation appears across a wide spectrum of economic development. At the upper extreme of the vertical axis, Antigua and Barbuda, Kuwait, and Guyana record the highest rates, all exceeding 0.6 kilograms per person per day, despite varying significantly in GDP. In the middle of the income distribution, countries like Sri Lanka, the Maldives, and Guatemala cluster between 0.3 and 0.4 kilograms. Large population centers occupy the lower-left quadrant; India and Indonesia, represented by the largest circles, sit below 0.1 kilograms with GDPs under $5,000. China and Brazil appear slightly higher, hovering near the 0.15 kilogram mark. Among high-income nations in Europe and North America, values generally range between 0.1 and 0.5 kilograms, with the United States and Germany positioned near 0.35 and 0.5 kilograms respectively, while Sweden and Romania sit lower near 0.1 kilograms. The visual distribution indicates that while some wealthy nations generate substantial waste, high GDP does not strictly predict the highest per capita rates, as several smaller island nations and specific developing economies exceed the output of major industrial powers. The chart displays a broad dispersion of points where the highest waste generators are not confined to the richest economic tier.
